b' Money Stuff
In negotiations, it is often helpful to have someone else, some \xe2\x80\x9cabsent principal,\xe2\x80\x9d to blame for your position. You go to a car dealership,

Art stuff

In negotiations, it is often helpful to have someone else, some \xe2\x80\x9cabsent principal,\xe2\x80\x9d to blame for your position. You go to a car dealership, the salesperson says \xe2\x80\x9cthis car costs $25,000,\xe2\x80\x9d you say \xe2\x80\x9cI want to pay $21,000,\xe2\x80\x9d she says \xe2\x80\x9cI like you, I want you in this car, but my boss won\xe2\x80\x99t let me go lower than $24,000,\xe2\x80\x9d you say \xe2\x80\x9c$22,000,\xe2\x80\x9d she says \xe2\x80\x9cI really want this to work out, let me check with my boss,\xe2\x80\x9d she goes into the break room and watches TikToks on her phone for five minutes, she comes back and says \xe2\x80\x9cmy boss is really mad at me but I talked him down to\xc2\xa0$23,500.\xe2\x80\x9d

The boss is a crutch, an excuse. The salesperson

  1. is adversarial to you \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0she wants to charge more, you want to pay less \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but
  2. wants you to feel like she\xe2\x80\x99s on your side, so you trust her and agree to her proposals.

The way to solve the tension\xc2\xa0is to set up some imaginary absent principal and offload all the adversarial feelings onto him. \xe2\x80\x9c I don\xe2\x80\x99t want to charge you this much,\xe2\x80\x9d the salesperson\xc2\xa0says or implies, \xe2\x80\x9cit\xe2\x80\x99s just my awful boss; really, you and I are in this together, both trying to get this deal past him.\xe2\x80\x9d And so you pay more than you wanted to, because you like her and feel like she\xe2\x80\x99s on your side, and because she has done all she can; she can\xe2\x80\x99t get the price any lower; that\xe2\x80\x99s her boss\xe2\x80\x99s fault.

This technique is pretty shopworn at the car dealership, and people have kind of\xc2\xa0stopped believing it, but there are better versions. There are, in the world, a lot of people who are what the financial industry would call \xe2\x80\x9cbroker-dealers\xe2\x80\x9d: Sometimes they act as brokers, finding buyers and sellers and negotiating a deal between them as pure agents, but but other times they act as dealers, owning stuff for their own account and buying or selling it\xc2\xa0at whatever price they can get. Sometimes they really do have some absent principal; other times, they are just trading for their own book.

It can be helpful, for these people, to create confusion about which role they are playing. For instance if you own some stuff and you want to sell it for $100, and your customer says \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ll pay $80,\xe2\x80\x9d it can be nice, for you, to say \xe2\x80\x9chang on, let me see if the seller is willing to go down to $95.\xe2\x80\x9d And then you put the customer on hold and watch TikToks, and\xc2\xa0you\xc2\xa0come back and say \xe2\x80\x9cI just talked to the seller, she\xe2\x80\x99s not happy and yelled at me a lot, but I was able to talk her down to $96, but that\xe2\x80\x99s the lowest she can go.\xe2\x80\x9d And the customer appreciates your good service and your\xc2\xa0efforts on his behalf, and feels like you and he are in this together, and agrees to pay $96. But there was no seller, or rather, you were the seller, and you just didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to go below $96. \xe2\x80\x9cThe seller\xe2\x80\x9d on the other line was just a crutch, an excuse. But the customer doesn\xe2\x80\x99t know that; he thinks that you were negotiating on his behalf, trying to get him the best possible deal. He doesn\xe2\x80\x99t know that you were on the other side, and that you were in fact trying to get him the worst possible deal that he\xe2\x80\x99d agree to.

In the financial industry this is somewhere between \xe2\x80\x9cfrowned upon\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9ca crime that you can go to prison for.\xe2\x80\x9d For a while, there was a wave of prosecutions of bond traders who did this stuff; some were acquitted and some convicted, but some of the convictions were reversed and this behavior is, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, somewhat ambiguously illegal. (Obviously not legal advice!) For instance, we once talked about a US Securities and Exchange Commission case against some Nomura traders who allegedly\xc2\xa0did stuff like this [1] :

On March 18, 2011, Peters learned that Nomura had purchased a block of the bond JPMMT 2006-A3 2A1 at 71-08. Shortly thereafter, Peters contacted a representative of a Nomura customer (\xe2\x80\x9cCustomer E\xe2\x80\x9d) and falsely claimed that the bond was being offered at 75-24, implying that a third-party, and not Nomura, owned the bond. Peters then elaborated on the misrepresentation, saying that the \xe2\x80\x9cseller\xe2\x80\x9d (who did not exist) probably had \xe2\x80\x9ca little room\xe2\x80\x9d (meaning, to lower the price) but that the bond\xe2\x80\x99s price likely would not fall by more than a point. The Customer E representative directed Peters to \xe2\x80\x9cFOK\xe2\x80\x9d (short for \xe2\x80\x9cfill or kill,\xe2\x80\x9d which is industry jargon for \xe2\x80\x9clast and final offer\xe2\x80\x9d) at 74-24, to which Peters replied, \xe2\x80\x9cFOK worked!\xe2\x80\x9d By misleading the Customer E representative about the fictional [offer] from the phantom seller, Peters extracted over $117,000 in additional profits for Nomura.

Customer E there thinks that Peters is doing a good job on his behalf, advising the customer on negotiating\xc2\xa0with the seller and talking the seller down to the lowest possible price. But in fact Peters is doing a good job on his own behalf, getting the customer to pay the highest possible price (to Peters) by pretending there is some absent seller on the other side.

This trade was for somewhat thinly traded residential mortgage-backed securities, by the way, which is important.\xc2\xa0One reason that this trick doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work that well anymore at car dealerships, and that it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work at all in\xc2\xa0the stock market, is that you need a customer who has no idea what the underlying thing is actually worth. You can\xe2\x80\x99t go around pretending to negotiate the price of a share of Apple Inc. stock, because the customer can just look on a screen and see its price, accurate to the penny and the second. You could go around pretending to negotiate the price of a Honda, but the internet has made\xc2\xa0car prices more transparent than they used to be. Little stubs of weird RMBS trade by appointment, and Nomura plausibly does have a much better idea what they cost than its customers do, so it can push up the price by doing a weird little pantomime negotiation.

Meanwhile in the art market prices are more or less entirely imaginary; a Gustav Klimt painting is worth, to a billionaire art collector, roughly $1 more than one other billionaire is willing to pay for it. So if you just make up a guy who thinks it\xe2\x80\x99s worth $185 million, maybe you can get your real billionaire customer to pay that much. The New York Times reports :

For example, in 2012, Bouvier told Rybolovlev\xe2\x80\x99s aide that the sellers of a Klimt, \xe2\x80\x9cWater Serpents II,\xe2\x80\x9d were looking for $190 million, but that he thought he could \xe2\x80\x9ctwist them to get 185,\xe2\x80\x9d according to court papers.

\xe2\x80\x9cThese negotiations did not happen,\xe2\x80\x9d Judge Furman wrote in his March opinion.

On the same day in 2012 that Bouvier wrote that email, one of his companies bought the painting for $126 million. Two days later, he invoiced Rybolovlev for $183.8 million.

Bouvier is Yves Bouvier, an art broker-dealer, and Rybolovlev is Dmitry Rybolovlev, \xe2\x80\x9ca Russian oligarch\xe2\x80\x9d and collector. Bouvier would sometimes buy art for his own account and then sell it to\xc2\xa0Rybolovlev at markups of tens of millions of dollars, which is a good trade if he\xe2\x80\x99s a dealer and bad work if he\xe2\x80\x99s a broker. The dispute is about which he was:

For years, Rybolovlev has accused Bouvier of defrauding him in that and dozens of other transactions by posing as an art adviser negotiating sales on Rybolovlev\xe2\x80\x99s behalf, when, in fact, he was secretly acting as an art dealer and often increasing the prices by tens of millions of dollars. \xe2\x80\xa6

Bouvier has long insisted he was always clear that he was operating not solely as an adviser, but also as an independent dealer. As evidence, he has put forward sales contracts for Rybolovlev\xe2\x80\x99s first few purchases as proof he was openly operating as a dealer, free to charge whatever price Rybolovlev was prepared to pay.

But the Russian collector has said that Bouvier\xe2\x80\x99s role evolved into that of commissioned adviser and agent, and that Bouvier pretended to play that role. He has emails in which Bouvier described negotiations with sellers that do not appear to have actually taken place.

We have talked about this case before , over the years; Rybolovlev has been pursuing Bouvier \xe2\x80\x9cin jurisdictions that included Monaco, Singapore, Hong Kong and Geneva,\xe2\x80\x9d but has mostly lost. This week in New York there will be a trial on\xc2\xa0Rybolovlev\xe2\x80\x99s claims against Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s, the auction house, which he says \xe2\x80\x9chelped Bouvier cheat him, in part by creating inflated valuations for the art that served to conceal Bouvier\xe2\x80\x99s large markups.\xe2\x80\x9d

Altmaning

Usually, the founder and chief executive officer of a startup would like to be able to raise money from investors while keeping complete control of the company, while the investors would prefer to have some control over how their money is used. Ultimately, if there is a sharp disagreement, the investors would like to be able to fire the founder and keep the company for themselves; the founder would like to be able to prevent that, and keep the company (and their money) for herself.

This is a real tension, both sides have good reasons for their positions \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0it\xe2\x80\x99s her vision, her blood, sweat and tears;\xc2\xa0it\xe2\x80\x99s their money \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and different startups strike the balance different ways. Some startups have dual-class stock structures and shareholder agreements that allow the founder to keep control of the board no matter how much outside money she raises. Other startups have single-class stock and shareholder agreements that give outside investors a lot of power. Generally, startups will have more founder-friendly structures if (1) they are in high demand (and can thus dictate terms to investors) and (2) their founders care about this stuff; some founders are sort of innocent and say \xe2\x80\x9cif I focus on doing a good job the governance will work itself out,\xe2\x80\x9d while other founders fight really hard for board control. And there are trends over time: When it is easy for startups to raise money, and hard for venture capitalists to get into deals, the founders get to dictate the terms, and the venture capitalists compete over who can be most founder-friendly. When capital is scarce, the providers of capital get to set the terms.

All of this is pretty straightforward stuff, a somewhat zero-sum battle between founders and investors for control. [2] It\xe2\x80\x99s the investors\xe2\x80\x99 money, it\xe2\x80\x99s the founder\xe2\x80\x99s vision, they each want protection, etc.

A weird innovation that OpenAI came up with was to introduce a third party that, technically, has\xc2\xa0absolute control of the company , and can ignore both what the founder/CEO wants and what the investors want. OpenAI has a board of (mostly) independent directors, and that board is founder/CEO Sam Altman\xe2\x80\x99s boss and does not answer to the investors: It is the board of directors of a nonprofit, who appoint themselves, and who have a fiduciary duty to the nonprofit\xe2\x80\x99s mission of \xe2\x80\x9cbuilding safe and beneficial artificial general intelligence for the benefit of humanity.\xe2\x80\x9d In this structure:

  • Altman has no power over the board: He was a board member until November, but he had only one vote, and now the board has kicked him off .
  • The investors have no power over the board: They had no voting rights at all, and OpenAI told them , in its operating agreement, that \xe2\x80\x9cit would be wise to view any investment in OpenAI Global, LLC in the spirit of a donation.\xe2\x80\x9d

And then the board fired Altman as CEO, and Altman and the investors were both extremely upset about this and entirely aligned with each other in wanting him back as CEO, and in the course of a few frantic days he did in fact come back as CEO. The board\xe2\x80\x99s practical power was limited by the fact that it was neither running the company nor supplying the money: It was just some outsiders with some votes; it could neither fund the development of OpenAI\xe2\x80\x99s large language models nor, you know, do that development itself. But the board\xe2\x80\x99s theoretical power was total.

Anyway here\xe2\x80\x99s a Wall Street Journal story about how, post-Open AI, founders want to get more control back from investors :

The entrepreneur world was stunned to see the board of hot artificial-intelligence company OpenAI fire Sam Altman just before Thanksgiving. He had been the face of one of the biggest successes of the year and suddenly he was out. In startup land, founders and advisers say they started discussing new ways to protect themselves.

Altman eventually made it back to OpenAI in a countercoup. But the tension at one of the country\xe2\x80\x99s biggest startups is playing out in a longstanding debate about who should control a burgeoning company. It is an inherent conflict in business, with founders wanting protection for their jobs while investors want it for their money.

Among startups, tougher economic conditions have recently given venture capitalists and investors the upper hand. After OpenAI, founders are going to try to regain their footing.

Sure, fine. \xe2\x80\x9cTougher economic conditions have recently given venture capitalists and investors the upper hand,\xe2\x80\x9d so terms are less founder-friendly, and founders would like them to be more founder-friendly, because that is an eternal and universal tension. And \xe2\x80\x9cwell look at what happened to Sam Altman\xe2\x80\x9d is, I suppose, an argument to make in that negotiation.

But it has nothing to do with anything! What happened to Sam Altman is not that his investors disagreed with his vision and fired him! What happened to Sam Altman is that OpenAI, almost uniquely, is an $86 billion startup whose governance terms are neither founder-friendly nor investor-friendly. They are nonprofit-board-friendly. That\xe2\x80\x99s so weird! If you\xe2\x80\x99re a tech startup, you don\xe2\x80\x99t need a nonprofit board! The number of tech startups that answer to a nonprofit board is very close to one! [3] If you don\xe2\x80\x99t have a nonprofit board, this whole problem doesn\xe2\x80\x99t exist!

On the other hand, if you are a founder looking to keep more control over your company, there is a lot to learn from OpenAI? The Journal story discusses the normal founder-friendly approaches:

Eric Ries, founder of the Long-Term Stock Exchange and something of a corporate governance guru and go-to mentor among the Silicon Valley set \xe2\x80\xa6 has a system of hurdles founders can set up that would make it harder for a board to move against a company\xe2\x80\x99s mission or management. The most protective moves, Ries and lawyers say, are implementing supervoting shares or dual-class shares, which give founders ultimate control over their companies. These structures create multiple classes of shares to give founders, and sometimes early employees or investors, voting control.

Okay sure but OpenAI has\xc2\xa0an operating agreement that (1) gives investors no votes at all and (2) tells them, in writing, that they should \xe2\x80\x9cview any\xc2\xa0investment \xe2\x80\xa6 in the spirit of a donation.\xe2\x80\x9d That\xe2\x80\x99s way better for a founder than dual-class stock, if the founder is sure she controls the board .

Carta

We talked yesterday about Carta, the startup that manages capitalization tables for other startups. I made the point that managing cap tables is a reasonable business \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0startups pay you a moderate amount of money to keep track of their shareholder lists \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but it is much less lucrative than the business of brokering secondary trades in startup stock , where people will pay you, like, 2% of the value of every trade to match up buyers and sellers. And managing cap tables would seem to be a huge advantage in getting the brokerage business: The lists of shareholders are generally secret , and if you have them (because you are in the business of keeping them), then you know who all the potential sellers are and can broker a lot of trades.

The problem is that the startups really do want those lists to be secret, and they will be mad if you go and use them to broker trades. And Carta did that: It came to a shareholder of a company whose cap table it manages, pitching that shareholder on selling his stock; the shareholder told the company and the company complained. And the result is that Carta is shutting down its brokerage business. The Financial Times reports :

The company\xe2\x80\x99s reputation has been battered by allegations that Carta staff used confidential information to solicit investors in start-ups to sell their stakes in the secondary market without consent from the start-ups themselves. ...

Henry Ward, the company\xe2\x80\x99s chief executive, said in a blog post on Monday night that the lacklustre performance and ultimate closure of the trading platform was \xe2\x80\x9cmy greatest failure and disappointment\xe2\x80\x9d.

\xe2\x80\x9cBecause we have the data, if we are trading secondaries, people will always worry that we are using the data, even if we are not. So we have decided to prioritise trust, and exit the secondary trading business,\xe2\x80\x9d he wrote.

What I said yesterday is that \xe2\x80\x9cwe\xe2\x80\x99ll give you an inexpensive service and monetize it by using the data for targeted advertising\xe2\x80\x9d is really the great business model of the 21st century, and that Carta\xe2\x80\x99s service would seem to generate very valuable data for targeted advertising. (The advertising is \xe2\x80\x9csell your shares of this hot startup,\xe2\x80\x9d targeted to actual shareholders.) It is rough, for a hot startup, to disavow that model. Perhaps it is a\xc2\xa0lesson in the dangers of monetizing too early.

Pillow talk

We talked last June about how the Covid-19 pandemic, and the associated rise in working from home, was good for work-from-home insider trading. Some percentage of insider trading involves people overhearing their partners\xe2\x80\x99 work calls at home, or looking at their partners\xe2\x80\x99 work email while their partners are in the bathroom, and then trading on the partners\xe2\x80\x99 work information. If everyone works from the office, there is a limited opportunity for this sort of thing. If everyone works from the bedroom they share with their partner, there is tons of opportunity for this sort of thing.

Today there\xe2\x80\x99s a Wall Street Journal A-hed treatment of the trend:

There is a rich history in securities fraud of \xe2\x80\x9cpillow talk\xe2\x80\x9d cases, in which insider traders glean confidential information from romantic partners. The Covid era offered a twist: Secrets weren\xe2\x80\x99t spilled in the bedroom or over a bottle of wine, but during the humdrum routine of two adults working from home.

\xe2\x80\x9cDuring Covid, there was an uptick in brazen conduct,\xe2\x80\x9d said Edward Imperatore, a defense lawyer at law firm Morrison & Foerster. \xe2\x80\x9cIn a work-from-home environment, people acted with more impunity.\xe2\x80\x9d

That strikes me as an obviously wrong analysis? It\xe2\x80\x99s not that people \xe2\x80\x9cacted with more impunity,\xe2\x80\x9d that they always had access to their partners\xe2\x80\x99 inside information but became lawless monster during Covid. It\xe2\x80\x99s that people had way, way, way, way, way more opportunity to access their partners\xe2\x80\x99 inside information, and the brazen ones made use of it.

The legal problem in these cases is: Did the insider tip her partner, hoping that he would trade on her work information, or did the partner betray the insider, stealing her information while she wasn\xe2\x80\x99t looking and selfishly jeopardizing her career so he could make a little money? [4] I think the answer is usually the latter, but not always:

One challenge for prosecutors is determining whether the partner who is privy to the information was in on the crime, said former federal prosecutor Brendan Quigley.

\xe2\x80\x9cDo they say, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, my God, I would never give information to my spouse or significant other?\xe2\x80\x99 It depends not only on what actually happened, but also on the nature of their relationship,\xe2\x80\x9d said Quigley, who prosecuted insider-trading cases in Manhattan.

For defense lawyers, pillow-talk cases can be difficult to handle at trial, particularly if one partner testifies against another. \xe2\x80\x9cTo a juror, this is the bad boyfriend,\xe2\x80\x9d said Imperatore, the defense attorney. \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99s acting badly in a relationship in a way that goes beyond the four corners of insider trading.\xe2\x80\x9d

Not surprisingly, many such relationships don\xe2\x80\x99t survive.

We have talked about this problem in the case of golf buddies: Sometimes, corporate executives talk about their work on the golf course, treating golf like therapy and expecting their golf buddies to keep it confidential. (In that case, if the golf buddy trades, he is guilty of insider trading, but the executive isn\xe2\x80\x99t.)\xc2\xa0Other times, corporate executives talk about their work on the golf course in order to tip their golf buddies , expecting those buddies to go trade on it. (In that case, they\xe2\x80\x99re both guilty of insider trading.)\xc2\xa0If you\xe2\x80\x99re not playing the round with them, it is hard to know which cases are which. Same, but more so, with romantic partners.

Shareholder Vote Exchange

We talked last\xc2\xa0Thursday about the Shareholder Vote Exchange, a business that lets retail shareholders sell their votes. My view was (1) obviously retail shareholders should sell their votes, which are worth nothing to them, but (2) who would buy them? Shareholder votes are worthless to everyone, except to activist hedge funds in very rare contested proxy fights (or buyers in very rare contested takeovers), and in the contested situations anyone who tries to buy votes is going to get very sued.

But SVE co-founder Preston Yadegar emailed me to point out the obvious use case I missed: Companies can buy their own shareholders\xe2\x80\x99 votes. \xe2\x80\x9cOur focus for now,\xe2\x80\x9d he writes,\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cis simply to help issuers achieve quorum (with higher chances of success and at a lower cost).\xe2\x80\x9d

We have talked a number of times in the\xc2\xa0last year or two about the baroque lengths that some companies have gone to to get their retail shareholders to vote. Most notably, AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc. was busily issuing shares to meme-stock investors, but then it ran out of authorized shares; it wanted to ask its shareholders to authorize more shares, but it concluded that it couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get enough votes, so it issued a novel form of preferred stock \xe2\x80\x94 APES \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0designed to rig the vote to overcome retail shareholder inertia. This led to lawsuits and was generally expensive and inconvenient. I wrote\xc2\xa0in 2022 , about AMC, that \xe2\x80\x9cif you are a meme-stock company with a retail-focused investor-relations function, you have to solve the problem of voting.\xe2\x80\x9d Possibly the simplest solution is to pay the retail investors a few bucks for their votes.

Things happen

Talent-Hungry Hedge Funds Seen Fueling Pod Shop M&A in 2024. How Wall Street Firms Are Warming to AI \xe2\x80\x94With Help From Silicon Valley.\xc2\xa0Microsoft\xe2\x80\x99s OpenAI Ties Face Potential EU Merger Probe . Once-Bankrupt Jefferson County Tests Muni-Bond Market With Mega Deal. Tinder Owner Match Draws Elliott Investment . China Deal Slump Forces Wall Street Banks to Cast a Wider Net. Ecstasy-for-Medicine Advocate Raises $100 Million. Elon Musk Criticizes WSJ Reporting on His Use of Illegal Drugs . \xe2\x80\x9c Mike Mayo was probably the only contestant at last year\xe2\x80\x99s powerlifting nationals who used to have an Alan Greenspan photo pinned to his apartment wall.\xe2\x80\x9d The over 150-year mystery of why urine is yellow has finally been cracked: study.

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] Tyler Peters, named in that block quote, was prosecuted criminally, but a jury acquitted him on all counts , and the SEC then dropped its charges against him . One of his co-defendants was convicted in the criminal trial, then a judge threw out his conviction , then an appeals court reinstated it .

[2] Somewhat zero-sum. Really what you want, in all of this, is to motivate the founder to do a maximally good job. A governance structure that gives her complete control no matter what is a bit demotivating, but so is a governance structure that micromanages and demoralizes her. But there is probably no universally correct setting that maximizes every founder\xe2\x80\x99s motivation.

[3] Oh go ahead and email me the examples if you want, but it\xe2\x80\x99s not, like, 17.

[4] The genders in this sentence are intentional. The Journal notes: \xe2\x80\x9cOne thing hasn\xe2\x80\x99t changed since the earliest days of pillow talk: It is usually the men who can\xe2\x80\x99t resist the urge to take advantage of their confidential information.\xe2\x80\x9d
